name: guidelines

entries.title: In.target: Target readership
entries.summary: All C++ programmers. This includes [programmers who might consider C](#s-cpl).

entries.title: In.aims: Aims
entries.summary: The purpose of this document is to help developers to adopt modern C++ (currently C++20 and C++17) and to achieve a more uniform style across code bases.

entries.title: In.0: Don't panic!
entries.summary: Take the time to understand the implications of a guideline rule on your program.

entries.title: In.not: Non-aims
entries.summary: The rules are not intended to be minimal or orthogonal.

entries.title: In.force: Enforcement
entries.summary: Rules with no enforcement are unmanageable for large code bases.

entries.title: In.struct: The structure of this document
entries.summary: Each rule (guideline, suggestion) can have several parts:

entries.title: In.sec: Major sections
entries.summary: * [In: Introduction](#s-introduction)

entries.title: P.1: Express ideas directly in code
entries.summary: Compilers don't read comments (or design documents) and neither do many programmers (consistently).

entries.title: P.2: Write in ISO Standard C++
entries.summary: This is a set of guidelines for writing ISO Standard C++.

entries.title: P.3: Express intent
entries.summary: Unless the intent of some code is stated (e.g., in names or comments), it is impossible to tell whether the code does what it is supposed to do.

entries.title: P.4: Ideally, a program should be statically type safe
entries.summary: Ideally, a program would be completely statically (compile-time) type safe.

entries.title: P.5: Prefer compile-time checking to run-time checking
entries.summary: Code clarity and performance.

entries.title: P.6: What cannot be checked at compile time should be checkable at run time
entries.summary: Leaving hard-to-detect errors in a program is asking for crashes and bad results.

entries.title: P.7: Catch run-time errors early
entries.summary: Avoid "mysterious" crashes.

entries.title: P.8: Don't leak any resources
entries.summary: Even a slow growth in resources will, over time, exhaust the availability of those resources.

entries.title: P.9: Don't waste time or space
entries.summary: This is C++.

entries.title: P.10: Prefer immutable data to mutable data
entries.summary: It is easier to reason about constants than about variables.

entries.title: P.11: Encapsulate messy constructs, rather than spreading through the code
entries.summary: Messy code is more likely to hide bugs and harder to write.

entries.title: P.12: Use supporting tools as appropriate
entries.summary: There are many things that are done better "by machine".

entries.title: P.13: Use support libraries as appropriate
entries.summary: Using a well-designed, well-documented, and well-supported library saves time and effort;

entries.title: I.1: Make interfaces explicit
entries.summary: Correctness. Assumptions not stated in an interface are easily overlooked and hard to test.

entries.title: I.2: Avoid non-`const` global variables
entries.summary: Non-`const` global variables hide dependencies and make the dependencies subject to unpredictable changes.

entries.title: I.3: Avoid singletons
entries.summary: Singletons are basically complicated global objects in disguise.

entries.title: I.4: Make interfaces precisely and strongly typed
entries.summary: Types are the simplest and best documentation, improve legibility due to their well-defined meaning, and are checked at compile time.

entries.title: I.5: State preconditions (if any)
entries.summary: Arguments have meaning that might constrain their proper use in the callee.

entries.title: I.6: Prefer `Expects()` for expressing preconditions
entries.summary: To make it clear that the condition is a precondition and to enable tool use.

entries.title: I.7: State postconditions
entries.summary: To detect misunderstandings about the result and possibly catch erroneous implementations.

entries.title: I.8: Prefer `Ensures()` for expressing postconditions
entries.summary: To make it clear that the condition is a postcondition and to enable tool use.

entries.title: I.9: If an interface is a template, document its parameters using concepts
entries.summary: Make the interface precisely specified and compile-time checkable in the (not so distant) future.

entries.title: I.10: Use exceptions to signal a failure to perform a required task
entries.summary: It should not be possible to ignore an error because that could leave the system or a computation in an undefined (or unexpected) state.

entries.title: I.11: Never transfer ownership by a raw pointer (`T*`) or reference (`T&`)
entries.summary: If there is any doubt whether the caller or the callee owns an object, leaks or premature destruction will occur.

entries.title: I.12: Declare a pointer that must not be null as `not_null`
entries.summary: To help avoid dereferencing `nullptr` errors.

entries.title: I.13: Do not pass an array as a single pointer
entries.summary: (pointer, size)-style interfaces are error-prone. Also, a plain pointer (to array) must rely on some convention to allow the callee to determine the size.

entries.title: I.22: Avoid complex initialization of global objects
entries.summary: Complex initialization can lead to undefined order of execution.

entries.title: I.23: Keep the number of function arguments low
entries.summary: Having many arguments opens opportunities for confusion. Passing lots of arguments is often costly compared to alternatives.

entries.title: I.24: Avoid adjacent parameters that can be invoked by the same arguments in either order with different meaning
entries.summary: Adjacent arguments of the same type are easily swapped by mistake.

entries.title: I.25: Prefer empty abstract classes as interfaces to class hierarchies
entries.summary: Abstract classes that are empty (have no non-static member data) are more likely to be stable than base classes with state.

entries.title: I.26: If you want a cross-compiler ABI, use a C-style subset
entries.summary: Different compilers implement different binary layouts for classes, exception handling, function names, and other implementation details.

entries.title: I.27: For stable library ABI, consider the Pimpl idiom
entries.summary: Because private data members participate in class layout and private member functions participate in overload resolution, changes to those

entries.title: I.30: Encapsulate rule violations
entries.summary: To keep code simple and safe.

entries.title: F.def: Function definitions
entries.summary: A function definition is a function declaration that also specifies the function's implementation, the function body.

entries.title: F.1: "Package" meaningful operations as carefully named functions
entries.summary: Factoring out common code makes code more readable, more likely to be reused, and limit errors from complex code.

entries.title: F.2: A function should perform a single logical operation
entries.summary: A function that performs a single operation is simpler to understand, test, and reuse.

entries.title: F.3: Keep functions short and simple
entries.summary: Large functions are hard to read, more likely to contain complex code, and more likely to have variables in larger than minimal scopes.

entries.title: F.4: If a function might have to be evaluated at compile time, declare it `constexpr`
entries.summary: `constexpr` is needed to tell the compiler to allow compile-time evaluation.

entries.title: F.5: If a function is very small and time-critical, declare it `inline`
entries.summary: Some optimizers are good at inlining without hints from the programmer, but don't rely on it.

entries.title: F.6: If your function must not throw, declare it `noexcept`
entries.summary: If an exception is not supposed to be thrown, the program cannot be assumed to cope with the error and should be terminated as soon as possible. Declaring a function `noexcept` helps optimizers by red

entries.title: F.7: For general use, take `T*` or `T&` arguments rather than smart pointers
entries.summary: Passing a smart pointer transfers or shares ownership and should only be used when ownership semantics are intended.

entries.title: F.8: Prefer pure functions
entries.summary: Pure functions are easier to reason about, sometimes easier to optimize (and even parallelize), and sometimes can be memoized.

entries.title: F.9: Unused parameters should be unnamed
entries.summary: Readability.

entries.title: F.10: If an operation can be reused, give it a name
entries.summary: Documentation, readability, opportunity for reuse.

entries.title: F.11: Use an unnamed lambda if you need a simple function object in one place only
entries.summary: That makes the code concise and gives better locality than alternatives.

entries.title: F.call: Parameter passing
entries.summary: There are a variety of ways to pass parameters to a function and to return values.

entries.title: F.15: Prefer simple and conventional ways of passing information
entries.summary: Using "unusual and clever" techniques causes surprises, slows understanding by other programmers, and encourages bugs.

entries.title: F.16: For "in" parameters, pass cheaply-copied types by value and others by reference to `const`
entries.summary: Both let the caller know that a function will not modify the argument, and both allow initialization by rvalues.

entries.title: F.17: For "in-out" parameters, pass by reference to non-`const`
entries.summary: This makes it clear to callers that the object is assumed to be modified.

entries.title: F.18: For "will-move-from" parameters, pass by `X&&` and `std::move` the parameter
entries.summary: It's efficient and eliminates bugs at the call site: `X&&` binds to rvalues, which requires an explicit `std::move` at the call site if passing an lvalue.

entries.title: F.19: For "forward" parameters, pass by `TP&&` and only `std::forward` the parameter
entries.summary: If the object is to be passed onward to other code and not directly used by this function, we want to make this function agnostic to the argument `const`-ness and rvalue-ness.

entries.title: F.20: For "out" output values, prefer return values to output parameters
entries.summary: A return value is self-documenting, whereas an `&` could be either in-out or out-only and is liable to be misused.

entries.title: F.21: To return multiple "out" values, prefer returning a struct
entries.summary: A return value is self-documenting as an "output-only" value.

entries.title: F.60: Prefer `T*` over `T&` when "no argument" is a valid option
entries.summary: A pointer (`T*`) can be a `nullptr` and a reference (`T&`) cannot, there is no valid "null reference".

entries.title: F.22: Use `T*` or `owner` to designate a single object
entries.summary: Readability: it makes the meaning of a plain pointer clear.

entries.title: F.23: Use a `not_null` to indicate that "null" is not a valid value
entries.summary: Clarity. A function with a `not_null<T>` parameter makes it clear that the caller of the function is responsible for any `nullptr` checks that might be necessary.

entries.title: F.24: Use a `span` or a `span_p` to designate a half-open sequence
entries.summary: Informal/non-explicit ranges are a source of errors.

entries.title: F.25: Use a `zstring` or a `not_null` to designate a C-style string
entries.summary: C-style strings are ubiquitous. They are defined by convention: zero-terminated arrays of characters.

entries.title: F.26: Use a `unique_ptr` to transfer ownership where a pointer is needed
entries.summary: Using `unique_ptr` is the cheapest way to pass a pointer safely.

entries.title: F.27: Use a `shared_ptr` to share ownership
entries.summary: Using `std::shared_ptr` is the standard way to represent shared ownership. That is, the last owner deletes the object.

entries.title: F.42: Return a `T*` to indicate a position (only)
entries.summary: That's what pointers are good for.

entries.title: F.43: Never (directly or indirectly) return a pointer or a reference to a local object
entries.summary: To avoid the crashes and data corruption that can result from the use of such a dangling pointer.

entries.title: F.44: Return a `T&` when copy is undesirable and "returning no object" isn't needed
entries.summary: The language guarantees that a `T&` refers to an object, so that testing for `nullptr` isn't necessary.

entries.title: F.45: Don't return a `T&&`
entries.summary: It's asking to return a reference to a destroyed temporary object.

entries.title: F.46: `int` is the return type for `main()`
entries.summary: It's a language rule, but violated through "language extensions" so often that it is worth mentioning.

entries.title: F.47: Return `T&` from assignment operators
entries.summary: The convention for operator overloads (especially on concrete types) is for

entries.title: F.48: Don't `return std::move(local)`
entries.summary: Returning a local variable implicitly moves it anyway.

entries.title: F.49: Don't return `const T`
entries.summary: It is not recommended to return a `const` value.

entries.title: F.50: Use a lambda when a function won't do (to capture local variables, or to write a local function)
entries.summary: Functions can't capture local variables or be defined at local scope; if you need those things, prefer a lambda where possible, and a handwritten function object where not. On the other hand, lambdas

entries.title: F.51: Where there is a choice, prefer default arguments over overloading
entries.summary: Default arguments simply provide alternative interfaces to a single implementation.

entries.title: F.52: Prefer capturing by reference in lambdas that will be used locally, including passed to algorithms
entries.summary: For efficiency and correctness, you nearly always want to capture by reference when using the lambda locally. This includes when writing or calling parallel algorithms that are local because they join

entries.title: F.53: Avoid capturing by reference in lambdas that will be used non-locally, including returned, stored on the heap, or passed to another thread
entries.summary: Pointers and references to locals shouldn't outlive their scope. Lambdas that capture by reference are just another place to store a reference to a local object, and shouldn't do so if they (or a copy

entries.title: F.54: When writing a lambda that captures `this` or any class data member, don't use `[=]` default capture
entries.summary: It's confusing. Writing `[=]` in a member function appears to capture by value, but actually captures data members by reference because it actually captures the invisible `this` pointer by value. If y

entries.title: F.55: Don't use `va_arg` arguments
entries.summary: Reading from a `va_arg` assumes that the correct type was actually passed.

entries.title: F.56: Avoid unnecessary condition nesting
entries.summary: Shallow nesting of conditions makes the code easier to follow. It also makes the intent clearer.

entries.title: C.1: Organize related data into structures (`struct`s or `class`es)
entries.summary: Ease of comprehension.

entries.title: C.2: Use `class` if the class has an invariant; use `struct` if the data members can vary independently
entries.summary: Readability.

entries.title: C.3: Represent the distinction between an interface and an implementation using a class
entries.summary: An explicit distinction between interface and implementation improves readability and simplifies maintenance.

entries.title: C.4: Make a function a member only if it needs direct access to the representation of a class
entries.summary: Less coupling than with member functions, fewer functions that can cause trouble by modifying object state, reduces the number of functions that need to be modified after a change in representation.

entries.title: C.5: Place helper functions in the same namespace as the class they support
entries.summary: A helper function is a function (usually supplied by the writer of a class) that does not need direct access to the representation of the class, yet is seen as part of the useful interface to the clas

entries.title: C.7: Don't define a class or enum and declare a variable of its type in the same statement
entries.summary: Mixing a type definition and the definition of another entity in the same declaration is confusing and unnecessary.

entries.title: C.8: Use `class` rather than `struct` if any member is non-public
entries.summary: Readability.

entries.title: C.9: Minimize exposure of members
entries.summary: Encapsulation.

entries.title: C.concrete: Concrete types
entries.summary: Concrete type rule summary:

entries.title: C.10: Prefer concrete types over class hierarchies
entries.summary: A concrete type is fundamentally simpler than a type in a class hierarchy:

entries.title: C.11: Make concrete types regular
entries.summary: Regular types are easier to understand and reason about than types that are not regular (irregularities require extra effort to understand and use).

entries.title: C.12: Don't make data members `const` or references in a copyable or movable type
entries.summary: `const` and reference data members are not useful in a copyable or movable type, and make such types difficult to use by making them at least partly uncopyable/unmovable for subtle reasons.

entries.title: C.13: If data member `B` uses another data member `A`, declare `A` before `B`
entries.summary: Data members are initialized in the order they are declared, and destroyed in the reverse order.

entries.title: C.ctor: Constructors, assignments, and destructors
entries.summary: These functions control the lifecycle of objects: creation, copy, move, and destruction.

entries.title: C.defop: Default Operations
entries.summary: By default, the language supplies the default operations with their default semantics.

entries.title: C.20: If you can avoid defining default operations, do
entries.summary: It's the simplest and gives the cleanest semantics.

entries.title: C.21: If you define or `=delete` any copy, move, or destructor function, define or `=delete` them all
entries.summary: The semantics of copy, move, and destruction are closely related, so if one needs to be declared, the odds are that others need consideration too.

entries.title: C.22: Make default operations consistent
entries.summary: The default operations are conceptually a matched set. Their semantics are interrelated.

entries.title: C.dtor: Destructors
entries.summary: "Does this class need a destructor?" is a surprisingly insightful design question.

entries.title: C.30: Define a destructor if a class needs an explicit action at object destruction
entries.summary: A destructor is implicitly invoked at the end of an object's lifetime.

entries.title: C.31: All resources acquired by a class must be released by the class's destructor
entries.summary: Prevention of resource leaks, especially in error cases.

entries.title: C.32: If a class has a raw pointer (`T*`) or reference (`T&`), consider whether it might be owning
entries.summary: There is a lot of code that is non-specific about ownership.

entries.title: C.33: If a class has an owning pointer member, define a destructor
entries.summary: An owned object must be `delete`d upon destruction of the object that owns it.

entries.title: C.35: A base class destructor should be either public and virtual, or protected and non-virtual
entries.summary: To prevent undefined behavior.

entries.title: C.36: A destructor must not fail
entries.summary: In general we do not know how to write error-free code if a destructor should fail.

entries.title: C.37: Make destructors `noexcept`
entries.summary: [A destructor must not fail](#rc-dtor-fail). If a destructor tries to exit with an exception, it's a bad design error and the program had better terminate.

entries.title: C.ctor: Constructors
entries.summary: A constructor defines how an object is initialized (constructed).

entries.title: C.40: Define a constructor if a class has an invariant
entries.summary: That's what constructors are for.

entries.title: C.41: A constructor should create a fully initialized object
entries.summary: A constructor establishes the invariant for a class. A user of a class should be able to assume that a constructed object is usable.

entries.title: C.42: If a constructor cannot construct a valid object, throw an exception
entries.summary: Leaving behind an invalid object is asking for trouble.

entries.title: C.43: Ensure that a copyable class has a default constructor
entries.summary: That is, ensure that if a concrete class is copyable it also satisfies the rest of "semiregular."

entries.title: C.44: Prefer default constructors to be simple and non-throwing
entries.summary: Being able to set a value to "the default" without operations that might fail simplifies error handling and reasoning about move operations.

entries.title: C.45: Don't define a default constructor that only initializes data members; use default member initializers instead
entries.summary: Using default member initializers lets the compiler generate the function for you. The compiler-generated function can be more efficient.

entries.title: C.46: By default, declare single-argument constructors explicit
entries.summary: To avoid unintended conversions.

entries.title: C.47: Define and initialize data members in the order of member declaration
entries.summary: To minimize confusion and errors. That is the order in which the initialization happens (independent of the order of member initializers).

entries.title: C.48: Prefer default member initializers to member initializers in constructors for constant initializers
entries.summary: Makes it explicit that the same value is expected to be used in all constructors. Avoids repetition. Avoids maintenance problems. It leads to the shortest and most efficient code.

entries.title: C.49: Prefer initialization to assignment in constructors
entries.summary: An initialization explicitly states that initialization, rather than assignment, is done and can be more elegant and efficient. Prevents "use before set" errors.

entries.title: C.50: Use a factory function if you need "virtual behavior" during initialization
entries.summary: If the state of a base class object must depend on the state of a derived part of the object, we need to use a virtual function (or equivalent) while minimizing the window of opportunity to misuse an

entries.title: C.51: Use delegating constructors to represent common actions for all constructors of a class
entries.summary: To avoid repetition and accidental differences.

entries.title: C.52: Use inheriting constructors to import constructors into a derived class that does not need further explicit initialization
entries.summary: If you need those constructors for a derived class, re-implementing them is tedious and error-prone.

entries.title: C.copy: Copy and move
entries.summary: Concrete types should generally be copyable, but interfaces in a class hierarchy should not.

entries.title: C.60: Make copy assignment non-`virtual`, take the parameter by `const&`, and return by non-`const&`
entries.summary: It is simple and efficient. If you want to optimize for rvalues, provide an overload that takes an `&&` (see [F.18](#rf-consume)).

entries.title: C.61: A copy operation should copy
entries.summary: That is the generally assumed semantics. After `x = y`, we should have `x == y`.

entries.title: C.62: Make copy assignment safe for self-assignment
entries.summary: If `x = x` changes the value of `x`, people will be surprised and bad errors will occur (often including leaks).

entries.title: C.63: Make move assignment non-`virtual`, take the parameter by `&&`, and return by non-`const&`
entries.summary: It is simple and efficient.

entries.title: C.64: A move operation should move and leave its source in a valid state
entries.summary: That is the generally assumed semantics.

entries.title: C.65: Make move assignment safe for self-assignment
entries.summary: If `x = x` changes the value of `x`, people will be surprised and bad errors can occur. However, people don't usually directly write a self-assignment that turns into a move, but it can occur. However

entries.title: C.66: Make move operations `noexcept`
entries.summary: A throwing move violates most people's reasonable assumptions.

entries.title: C.67: A polymorphic class should suppress public copy/move
entries.summary: A *polymorphic class* is a class that defines or inherits at least one virtual function. It is likely that it will be used as a base class for other derived classes with polymorphic behavior. If it is

entries.title: C.other: Other default operation rules
entries.summary: In addition to the operations for which the language offers default implementations,

entries.title: C.80: Use `=default` if you have to be explicit about using the default semantics
entries.summary: The compiler is more likely to get the default semantics right and you cannot implement these functions better than the compiler.

entries.title: C.81: Use `=delete` when you want to disable default behavior (without wanting an alternative)
entries.summary: In a few cases, a default operation is not desirable.

entries.title: C.82: Don't call virtual functions in constructors and destructors
entries.summary: The function called will be that of the object constructed so far, rather than a possibly overriding function in a derived class.

entries.title: C.83: For value-like types, consider providing a `noexcept` swap function
entries.summary: A `swap` can be handy for implementing a number of idioms, from smoothly moving objects around to implementing assignment easily to providing a guaranteed commit function that enables strongly error-s

entries.title: C.84: A `swap` function must not fail
entries.summary: `swap` is widely used in ways that are assumed never to fail and programs cannot easily be written to work correctly in the presence of a failing `swap`. The standard-library containers and algorithms

entries.title: C.85: Make `swap` `noexcept`
entries.summary: [A `swap` must not fail](#rc-swap-fail).

entries.title: C.86: Make `==` symmetric with respect to operand types and `noexcept`
entries.summary: Asymmetric treatment of operands is surprising and a source of errors where conversions are possible.

entries.title: C.87: Beware of `==` on base classes
entries.summary: It is really hard to write a foolproof and useful `==` for a hierarchy.

entries.title: C.89: Make a `hash` `noexcept`
entries.summary: Users of hashed containers use hash indirectly and don't expect simple access to throw.

entries.title: C.90: Rely on constructors and assignment operators, not `memset` and `memcpy`
entries.summary: The standard C++ mechanism to construct an instance of a type is to call its constructor. As specified in guideline [C.41](#rc-complete): a constructor should create a fully initialized object. No add

entries.title: C.con: Containers and other resource handles
entries.summary: A container is an object holding a sequence of objects of some type; `std::vector` is the archetypical container.

entries.title: C.100: Follow the STL when defining a container
entries.summary: The STL containers are familiar to most C++ programmers and a fundamentally sound design.

entries.title: C.101: Give a container value semantics
entries.summary: Regular objects are simpler to think and reason about than irregular ones.

entries.title: C.102: Give a container move operations
entries.summary: Containers tend to get large; without a move constructor and a copy constructor an object can be

entries.title: C.103: Give a container an initializer list constructor
entries.summary: People expect to be able to initialize a container with a set of values.

entries.title: C.104: Give a container a default constructor that sets it to empty
entries.summary: To make it `Regular`.

entries.title: C.109: If a resource handle has pointer semantics, provide `*` and `->`
entries.summary: That's what is expected from pointers.

entries.title: C.lambdas: Function objects and lambdas
entries.summary: A function object is an object supplying an overloaded `()` so that you can call it.

entries.title: C.hier: Class hierarchies (OOP)
entries.summary: A class hierarchy is constructed to represent a set of hierarchically organized concepts (only).

entries.title: C.120: Use class hierarchies to represent concepts with inherent hierarchical structure (only)
entries.summary: Direct representation of ideas in code eases comprehension and maintenance. Make sure the idea represented in the base class exactly matches all derived types and there is not a better way to express

entries.title: C.121: If a base class is used as an interface, make it a pure abstract class
entries.summary: A class is more stable (less brittle) if it does not contain data.

entries.title: C.122: Use abstract classes as interfaces when complete separation of interface and implementation is needed
entries.summary: Such as on an ABI (link) boundary.

entries.title: C.126: An abstract class typically doesn't need a user-written constructor
entries.summary: An abstract class typically does not have any data for a constructor to initialize.

entries.title: C.127: A class with a virtual function should have a virtual or protected destructor
entries.summary: A class with a virtual function is usually (and in general) used via a pointer to base. Usually, the last user has to call delete on a pointer to base, often via a smart pointer to base, so the destru

entries.title: C.128: Virtual functions should specify exactly one of `virtual`, `override`, or `final`
entries.summary: Readability.

entries.title: C.129: When designing a class hierarchy, distinguish between implementation inheritance and interface inheritance
entries.summary: Implementation details in an interface make the interface brittle;

entries.title: C.130: For making deep copies of polymorphic classes prefer a virtual `clone` function instead of public copy construction/assignment
entries.summary: Copying a polymorphic class is discouraged due to the slicing problem, see [C.67](#rc-copy-virtual). If you really need copy semantics, copy deeply: Provide a virtual `clone` function that will copy t

entries.title: C.131: Avoid trivial getters and setters
entries.summary: A trivial getter or setter adds no semantic value; the data item could just as well be `public`.

entries.title: C.132: Don't make a function `virtual` without reason
entries.summary: Redundant `virtual` increases run-time and object-code size.

entries.title: C.133: Avoid `protected` data
entries.summary: **Alternative formulation**: Make member data `public` or (preferably) `private`.

entries.title: C.134: Ensure all non-`const` data members have the same access level
entries.summary: Prevention of logical confusion leading to errors.

entries.title: C.135: Use multiple inheritance to represent multiple distinct interfaces
entries.summary: Not all classes will necessarily support all interfaces, and not all callers will necessarily want to deal with all operations.

entries.title: C.136: Use multiple inheritance to represent the union of implementation attributes
entries.summary: Some forms of mixins have state and often operations on that state.

entries.title: C.137: Use `virtual` bases to avoid overly general base classes
entries.summary: Allow separation of shared data and interface.

entries.title: C.138: Create an overload set for a derived class and its bases with `using`
entries.summary: Without a using declaration, member functions in the derived class hide the entire inherited overload sets.

entries.title: C.139: Use `final` on classes sparingly
entries.summary: Capping a hierarchy with `final` classes is rarely needed for logical reasons and can be damaging to the extensibility of a hierarchy.

entries.title: C.140: Do not provide different default arguments for a virtual function and an overrider
entries.summary: That can cause confusion: An overrider does not inherit default arguments.

entries.title: C.145: Access polymorphic objects through pointers and references
entries.summary: If you have a class with a virtual function, you don't (in general) know which class provided the function to be used.

entries.title: C.146: Use `dynamic_cast` where class hierarchy navigation is unavoidable
entries.summary: `dynamic_cast` is checked at run time.

entries.title: C.147: Use `dynamic_cast` to a reference type when failure to find the required class is considered an error
entries.summary: Casting to a reference expresses that you intend to end up with a valid object, so the cast must succeed. `dynamic_cast` will then throw if it does not succeed.

entries.title: C.148: Use `dynamic_cast` to a pointer type when failure to find the required class is considered a valid alternative
entries.summary: The `dynamic_cast` conversion allows to test whether a pointer is pointing at a polymorphic object that has a given class in its hierarchy. Since failure to find the class merely returns a null value,

entries.title: C.149: Use `unique_ptr` or `shared_ptr` to avoid forgetting to `delete` objects created using `new`
entries.summary: Avoid resource leaks.

entries.title: C.150: Use `make_unique()` to construct objects owned by `unique_ptr`s
entries.summary: See [R.23](#rr-make_unique)

entries.title: C.151: Use `make_shared()` to construct objects owned by `shared_ptr`s
entries.summary: See [R.22](#rr-make_shared)

entries.title: C.152: Never assign a pointer to an array of derived class objects to a pointer to its base
entries.summary: Subscripting the resulting base pointer will lead to invalid object access and probably to memory corruption.

entries.title: C.153: Prefer virtual function to casting
entries.summary: A virtual function call is safe, whereas casting is error-prone.

entries.title: C.over: Overloading and overloaded operators
entries.summary: You can overload ordinary functions, function templates, and operators.

entries.title: C.160: Define operators primarily to mimic conventional usage
entries.summary: Minimize surprises.

entries.title: C.161: Use non-member functions for symmetric operators
entries.summary: If you use member functions, you need two.

entries.title: C.162: Overload operations that are roughly equivalent
entries.summary: Having different names for logically equivalent operations on different argument types is confusing, leads to encoding type information in function names, and inhibits generic programming.

entries.title: C.163: Overload only for operations that are roughly equivalent
entries.summary: Having the same name for logically different functions is confusing and leads to errors when using generic programming.

entries.title: C.164: Avoid implicit conversion operators
entries.summary: Implicit conversions can be essential (e.g., `double` to `int`) but often cause surprises (e.g., `String` to C-style string).

entries.title: C.165: Use `using` for customization points
entries.summary: To find function objects and functions defined in a separate namespace to "customize" a common function.

entries.title: C.166: Overload unary `&` only as part of a system of smart pointers and references
entries.summary: The `&` operator is fundamental in C++.

entries.title: C.167: Use an operator for an operation with its conventional meaning
entries.summary: Readability. Convention. Reusability. Support for generic code

entries.title: C.168: Define overloaded operators in the namespace of their operands
entries.summary: Readability.

entries.title: C.170: If you feel like overloading a lambda, use a generic lambda
entries.summary: You cannot overload by defining two different lambdas with the same name.

entries.title: C.union: Unions
entries.summary: A `union` is a `struct` where all members start at the same address so that it can hold only one member at a time.

entries.title: C.180: Use `union`s to save memory
entries.summary: A `union` allows a single piece of memory to be used for different types of objects at different times.

entries.title: C.181: Avoid "naked" `union`s
entries.summary: A *naked union* is a union without an associated indicator which member (if any) it holds,

entries.title: C.182: Use anonymous `union`s to implement tagged unions
entries.summary: A well-designed tagged union is type safe.

entries.title: C.183: Don't use a `union` for type punning
entries.summary: It is undefined behavior to read a `union` member with a different type from the one with which it was written.

entries.title: Enum.1: Prefer enumerations over macros
entries.summary: Macros do not obey scope and type rules. Also, macro names are removed during preprocessing and so usually don't appear in tools like debuggers.

entries.title: Enum.2: Use enumerations to represent sets of related named constants
entries.summary: An enumeration shows the enumerators to be related and can be a named type.

entries.title: Enum.3: Prefer class enums over "plain" enums
entries.summary: To minimize surprises: traditional enums convert to int too readily.

entries.title: Enum.4: Define operations on enumerations for safe and simple use
entries.summary: Convenience of use and avoidance of errors.

entries.title: Enum.5: Don't use `ALL_CAPS` for enumerators
entries.summary: Avoid clashes with macros.

entries.title: Enum.6: Avoid unnamed enumerations
entries.summary: If you can't name an enumeration, the values are not related

entries.title: Enum.7: Specify the underlying type of an enumeration only when necessary
entries.summary: The default is the easiest to read and write.

entries.title: Enum.8: Specify enumerator values only when necessary
entries.summary: It's the simplest.

entries.title: R.1: Manage resources automatically using resource handles and RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization)
entries.summary: To avoid leaks and the complexity of manual resource management.

entries.title: R.2: In interfaces, use raw pointers to denote individual objects (only)
entries.summary: Arrays are best represented by a container type (e.g., `vector` (owning)) or a `span` (non-owning).

entries.title: R.3: A raw pointer (a `T*`) is non-owning
entries.summary: There is nothing (in the C++ standard or in most code) to say otherwise and most raw pointers are non-owning.

entries.title: R.4: A raw reference (a `T&`) is non-owning
entries.summary: There is nothing (in the C++ standard or in most code) to say otherwise and most raw references are non-owning.

entries.title: R.5: Prefer scoped objects, don't heap-allocate unnecessarily
entries.summary: A scoped object is a local object, a global object, or a member.

entries.title: R.6: Avoid non-`const` global variables
entries.summary: See [I.2](#ri-global)

entries.title: R.10: Avoid `malloc()` and `free()`
entries.summary: `malloc()` and `free()` do not support construction and destruction, and do not mix well with `new` and `delete`.

entries.title: R.11: Avoid calling `new` and `delete` explicitly
entries.summary: The pointer returned by `new` should belong to a resource handle (that can call `delete`).

entries.title: R.12: Immediately give the result of an explicit resource allocation to a manager object
entries.summary: If you don't, an exception or a return might lead to a leak.

entries.title: R.13: Perform at most one explicit resource allocation in a single expression statement
entries.summary: If you perform two explicit resource allocations in one statement, you could leak resources because the order of evaluation of many subexpressions, including function arguments, is unspecified.

entries.title: R.14: Avoid `[]` parameters, prefer `span`
entries.summary: An array decays to a pointer, thereby losing its size, opening the opportunity for range errors.

entries.title: R.15: Always overload matched allocation/deallocation pairs
entries.summary: Otherwise you get mismatched operations and chaos.

entries.title: R.20: Use `unique_ptr` or `shared_ptr` to represent ownership
entries.summary: They can prevent resource leaks.

entries.title: R.21: Prefer `unique_ptr` over `shared_ptr` unless you need to share ownership
entries.summary: A `unique_ptr` is conceptually simpler and more predictable (you know when destruction happens) and faster (you don't implicitly maintain a use count).

entries.title: R.22: Use `make_shared()` to make `shared_ptr`s
entries.summary: `make_shared` gives a more concise statement of the construction.

entries.title: R.23: Use `make_unique()` to make `unique_ptr`s
entries.summary: `make_unique` gives a more concise statement of the construction.

entries.title: R.24: Use `std::weak_ptr` to break cycles of `shared_ptr`s
entries.summary: `shared_ptr`s rely on use counting and the use count for a cyclic structure never goes to zero, so we need a mechanism to

entries.title: R.30: Take smart pointers as parameters only to explicitly express lifetime semantics
entries.summary: See [F.7](#rf-smart).

entries.title: R.31: If you have non-`std` smart pointers, follow the basic pattern from `std`
entries.summary: The rules in the following section also work for other kinds of third-party and custom smart pointers and are very useful for diagnosing common smart pointer errors that cause performance and correctn

entries.title: R.32: Take a `unique_ptr` parameter to express that a function assumes ownership of a `widget`
entries.summary: Using `unique_ptr` in this way both documents and enforces the function call's ownership transfer.

entries.title: R.33: Take a `unique_ptr&` parameter to express that a function reseats the `widget`
entries.summary: Using `unique_ptr` in this way both documents and enforces the function call's reseating semantics.

entries.title: R.34: Take a `shared_ptr` parameter to express shared ownership
entries.summary: This makes the function's ownership sharing explicit.

entries.title: R.35: Take a `shared_ptr&` parameter to express that a function might reseat the shared pointer
entries.summary: This makes the function's reseating explicit.

entries.title: R.36: Take a `const shared_ptr&` parameter to express that it might retain a reference count to the object ???
entries.summary: This makes the function's ??? explicit.

entries.title: R.37: Do not pass a pointer or reference obtained from an aliased smart pointer
entries.summary: Violating this rule is the number one cause of losing reference counts and finding yourself with a dangling pointer.

entries.title: ES.1: Prefer the standard library to other libraries and to "handcrafted code"
entries.summary: Code using a library can be much easier to write than code working directly with language features, much shorter, tend to be of a higher level of abstraction, and the library code is presumably alread

entries.title: ES.2: Prefer suitable abstractions to direct use of language features
entries.summary: A "suitable abstraction" (e.g., library or class) is closer to the application concepts than the bare language, leads to shorter and clearer code, and is likely to be better tested.

entries.title: ES.3: Don't repeat yourself, avoid redundant code
entries.summary: Duplicated or otherwise redundant code obscures intent, makes it harder to understand the logic, and makes maintenance harder, among other problems. It often arises from cut-and-paste programming.

entries.title: ES.dcl: Declarations
entries.summary: A declaration is a statement. A declaration introduces a name into a scope and might cause the construction of a named object.

entries.title: ES.5: Keep scopes small
entries.summary: Readability. Minimize resource retention. Avoid accidental misuse of value.

entries.title: ES.6: Declare names in for-statement initializers and conditions to limit scope
entries.summary: Readability.

entries.title: ES.7: Keep common and local names short, and keep uncommon and non-local names longer
entries.summary: Readability. Lowering the chance of clashes between unrelated non-local names.

entries.title: ES.8: Avoid similar-looking names
entries.summary: Code clarity and readability. Too-similar names slow down comprehension and increase the likelihood of error.

entries.title: ES.9: Avoid `ALL_CAPS` names
entries.summary: Such names are commonly used for macros. Thus, `ALL_CAPS` name are vulnerable to unintended macro substitution.

entries.title: ES.10: Declare one name (only) per declaration
entries.summary: One declaration per line increases readability and avoids mistakes related to

entries.title: ES.11: Use `auto` to avoid redundant repetition of type names
entries.summary: * Simple repetition is tedious and error-prone.

entries.title: ES.12: Do not reuse names in nested scopes
entries.summary: It is easy to get confused about which variable is used.

entries.title: ES.20: Always initialize an object
entries.summary: Avoid used-before-set errors and their associated undefined behavior.

entries.title: ES.21: Don't introduce a variable (or constant) before you need to use it
entries.summary: Readability. To limit the scope in which the variable can be used.

entries.title: ES.22: Don't declare a variable until you have a value to initialize it with
entries.summary: Readability. Limit the scope in which a variable can be used. Don't risk used-before-set. Initialization is often more efficient than assignment.

entries.title: ES.23: Prefer the `{}`-initializer syntax
entries.summary: Prefer `{}`. The rules for `{}` initialization are simpler, more general, less ambiguous, and safer than for other forms of initialization.

entries.title: ES.24: Use a `unique_ptr` to hold pointers
entries.summary: Using `std::unique_ptr` is the simplest way to avoid leaks. It is reliable, it

entries.title: ES.25: Declare an object `const` or `constexpr` unless you want to modify its value later on
entries.summary: That way you can't change the value by mistake. That way might offer the compiler optimization opportunities.

entries.title: ES.26: Don't use a variable for two unrelated purposes
entries.summary: Readability and safety.

entries.title: ES.27: Use `std::array` or `stack_array` for arrays on the stack
entries.summary: They are readable and don't implicitly convert to pointers.

entries.title: ES.28: Use lambdas for complex initialization, especially of `const` variables
entries.summary: It nicely encapsulates local initialization, including cleaning up scratch variables needed only for the initialization, without needing to create a needless non-local yet non-reusable function. It al

entries.title: ES.30: Don't use macros for program text manipulation
entries.summary: Macros are a major source of bugs.

entries.title: ES.31: Don't use macros for constants or "functions"
entries.summary: Macros are a major source of bugs.

entries.title: ES.32: Use `ALL_CAPS` for all macro names
entries.summary: Convention. Readability. Distinguishing macros.

entries.title: ES.33: If you must use macros, give them unique names
entries.summary: Macros do not obey scope rules.

entries.title: ES.34: Don't define a (C-style) variadic function
entries.summary: Not type safe.

entries.title: ES.expr: Expressions
entries.summary: Expressions manipulate values.

entries.title: ES.40: Avoid complicated expressions
entries.summary: Complicated expressions are error-prone.

entries.title: ES.41: If in doubt about operator precedence, parenthesize
entries.summary: Avoid errors. Readability. Not everyone has the operator table memorized.

entries.title: ES.42: Keep use of pointers simple and straightforward
entries.summary: Complicated pointer manipulation is a major source of errors.

entries.title: ES.43: Avoid expressions with undefined order of evaluation
entries.summary: You have no idea what such code does. Portability.

entries.title: ES.44: Don't depend on order of evaluation of function arguments
entries.summary: Because that order is unspecified.

entries.title: ES.45: Avoid "magic constants"; use symbolic constants
entries.summary: Unnamed constants embedded in expressions are easily overlooked and often hard to understand:

entries.title: ES.46: Avoid lossy (narrowing, truncating) arithmetic conversions
entries.summary: A narrowing conversion destroys information, often unexpectedly so.

entries.title: ES.47: Use `nullptr` rather than `0` or `NULL`
entries.summary: Readability. Minimize surprises: `nullptr` cannot be confused with an

entries.title: ES.48: Avoid casts
entries.summary: Casts are a well-known source of errors and make some optimizations unreliable.

entries.title: ES.49: If you must use a cast, use a named cast
entries.summary: Readability. Error avoidance.

entries.title: ES.50: Don't cast away `const`
entries.summary: It makes a lie out of `const`.

entries.title: ES.55: Avoid the need for range checking
entries.summary: Constructs that cannot overflow do not overflow (and usually run faster):

entries.title: ES.56: Write `std::move()` only when you need to explicitly move an object to another scope
entries.summary: We move, rather than copy, to avoid duplication and for improved performance.

entries.title: ES.60: Avoid `new` and `delete` outside resource management functions
entries.summary: Direct resource management in application code is error-prone and tedious.

entries.title: ES.61: Delete arrays using `delete[]` and non-arrays using `delete`
entries.summary: That's what the language requires, and mismatches can lead to resource release errors and/or memory corruption.

entries.title: ES.62: Don't compare pointers into different arrays
entries.summary: The result of doing so is undefined.

entries.title: ES.63: Don't slice
entries.summary: Slicing -- that is, copying only part of an object using assignment or initialization -- most often leads to errors because

entries.title: ES.64: Use the `T{e}`notation for construction
entries.summary: The `T{e}` construction syntax makes it explicit that construction is desired.

entries.title: ES.65: Don't dereference an invalid pointer
entries.summary: Dereferencing an invalid pointer, such as `nullptr`, is undefined behavior, typically leading to immediate crashes,

entries.title: ES.stmt: Statements
entries.summary: Statements control the flow of control (except for function calls and exception throws, which are expressions).

entries.title: ES.70: Prefer a `switch`-statement to an `if`-statement when there is a choice
entries.summary: * Readability.

entries.title: ES.71: Prefer a range-`for`-statement to a `for`-statement when there is a choice
entries.summary: Readability. Error prevention. Efficiency.

entries.title: ES.72: Prefer a `for`-statement to a `while`-statement when there is an obvious loop variable
entries.summary: Readability: the complete logic of the loop is visible "up front". The scope of the loop variable can be limited.

entries.title: ES.73: Prefer a `while`-statement to a `for`-statement when there is no obvious loop variable
entries.summary: Readability.

entries.title: ES.74: Prefer to declare a loop variable in the initializer part of a `for`-statement
entries.summary: See [ES.6](#res-cond)

entries.title: ES.75: Avoid `do`-statements
entries.summary: Readability, avoidance of errors.

entries.title: ES.76: Avoid `goto`
entries.summary: Readability, avoidance of errors. There are better control structures for humans; `goto` is for machine generated code.

entries.title: ES.77: Minimize the use of `break` and `continue` in loops
entries.summary: In a non-trivial loop body, it is easy to overlook a `break` or a `continue`.

entries.title: ES.78: Don't rely on implicit fallthrough in `switch` statements
entries.summary: Always end a non-empty `case` with a `break`. Accidentally leaving out a `break` is a fairly common bug.

entries.title: ES.79: Use `default` to handle common cases (only)
entries.summary: Code clarity.

entries.title: ES.84: Don't try to declare a local variable with no name
entries.summary: There is no such thing.

entries.title: ES.85: Make empty statements visible
entries.summary: Readability.

entries.title: ES.86: Avoid modifying loop control variables inside the body of raw for-loops
entries.summary: The loop control up front should enable correct reasoning about what is happening inside the loop. Modifying loop counters in both the iteration-expression and inside the body of the loop is a perenni

entries.title: ES.87: Don't add redundant `==` or `!=` to conditions
entries.summary: Doing so avoids verbosity and eliminates some opportunities for mistakes.

entries.title: ES.100: Don't mix signed and unsigned arithmetic
entries.summary: Avoid wrong results.

entries.title: ES.101: Use unsigned types for bit manipulation
entries.summary: Unsigned types support bit manipulation without surprises from sign bits.

entries.title: ES.102: Use signed types for arithmetic
entries.summary: Because most arithmetic is assumed to be signed;

entries.title: ES.103: Don't overflow
entries.summary: Overflow usually makes your numeric algorithm meaningless.

entries.title: ES.104: Don't underflow
entries.summary: Decrementing a value beyond a minimum value can lead to memory corruption and undefined behavior.

entries.title: ES.105: Don't divide by integer zero
entries.summary: The result is undefined and probably a crash.

entries.title: ES.106: Don't try to avoid negative values by using `unsigned`
entries.summary: Choosing `unsigned` implies many changes to the usual behavior of integers, including modular arithmetic,

entries.title: ES.107: Don't use `unsigned` for subscripts, prefer `gsl::index`
entries.summary: To avoid signed/unsigned confusion.

entries.title: Per.1: Don't optimize without reason
entries.summary: If there is no need for optimization, the main result of the effort will be more errors and higher maintenance costs.

entries.title: Per.2: Don't optimize prematurely
entries.summary: Elaborately optimized code is usually larger and harder to change than unoptimized code.

entries.title: Per.3: Don't optimize something that's not performance critical
entries.summary: Optimizing a non-performance-critical part of a program has no effect on system performance.

entries.title: Per.4: Don't assume that complicated code is necessarily faster than simple code
entries.summary: Simple code can be very fast. Optimizers sometimes do marvels with simple code

entries.title: Per.5: Don't assume that low-level code is necessarily faster than high-level code
entries.summary: Low-level code sometimes inhibits optimizations. Optimizers sometimes do marvels with high-level code.

entries.title: Per.6: Don't make claims about performance without measurements
entries.summary: The field of performance is littered with myth and bogus folklore.

entries.title: Per.7: Design to enable optimization
entries.summary: Because we often need to optimize the initial design.

entries.title: Per.10: Rely on the static type system
entries.summary: Type violations, weak types (e.g. `void*`s), and low-level code (e.g., manipulation of sequences as individual bytes) make the job of the optimizer much harder. Simple code often optimizes better than

entries.title: Per.11: Move computation from run time to compile time
entries.summary: To decrease code size and run time.

entries.title: Per.12: Eliminate redundant aliases
Per.13: Eliminate redundant indirections
Per.14: Minimize the number of allocations and deallocations
Per.15: Do not allocate on a critical branch
Per.16: Use compact data structures
entries.summary: Performance is typically dominated by memory access times.

entries.title: Per.17: Declare the most used member of a time-critical struct first
Per.18: Space is time
entries.summary: Performance is typically dominated by memory access times.

entries.title: Per.19: Access memory predictably
entries.summary: Performance is very sensitive to cache performance, and cache algorithms favor simple (usually linear) access to adjacent data.

entries.title: Per.30: Avoid context switches on the critical path
CP.1: Assume that your code will run as part of a multi-threaded program
entries.summary: It's hard to be certain that concurrency isn't used now or won't be used sometime in the future.

entries.title: CP.2: Avoid data races
entries.summary: Unless you do, nothing is guaranteed to work and subtle errors will persist.

entries.title: CP.3: Minimize explicit sharing of writable data
entries.summary: If you don't share writable data, you can't have a data race.

entries.title: CP.4: Think in terms of tasks, rather than threads
entries.summary: A `thread` is an implementation concept, a way of thinking about the machine.

entries.title: CP.8: Don't try to use `volatile` for synchronization
entries.summary: In C++, unlike some other languages, `volatile` does not provide atomicity, does not synchronize between threads,

entries.title: CP.9: Whenever feasible use tools to validate your concurrent code
entries.summary: Experience shows that concurrent code is exceptionally hard to get right

entries.title: CP.con: Concurrency
entries.summary: This section focuses on relatively ad-hoc uses of multiple threads communicating through shared data.

entries.title: CP.20: Use RAII, never plain `lock()`/`unlock()`
entries.summary: Avoids nasty errors from unreleased locks.

entries.title: CP.21: Use `std::lock()` or `std::scoped_lock` to acquire multiple `mutex`es
entries.summary: To avoid deadlocks on multiple `mutex`es.

entries.title: CP.22: Never call unknown code while holding a lock (e.g., a callback)
entries.summary: If you don't know what a piece of code does, you are risking deadlock.

entries.title: CP.23: Think of a joining `thread` as a scoped container
entries.summary: To maintain pointer safety and avoid leaks, we need to consider what pointers are used by a `thread`.

entries.title: CP.24: Think of a `thread` as a global container
entries.summary: To maintain pointer safety and avoid leaks, we need to consider what pointers are used by a `thread`.

entries.title: CP.25: Prefer `gsl::joining_thread` over `std::thread`
entries.summary: A `joining_thread` is a thread that joins at the end of its scope.

entries.title: CP.26: Don't `detach()` a thread
entries.summary: Often, the need to outlive the scope of its creation is inherent in the `thread`s task,

entries.title: CP.31: Pass small amounts of data between threads by value, rather than by reference or pointer
entries.summary: A small amount of data is cheaper to copy and access than to share it using some locking mechanism.

entries.title: CP.32: To share ownership between unrelated `thread`s use `shared_ptr`
entries.summary: If threads are unrelated (that is, not known to be in the same scope or one within the lifetime of the other)

entries.title: CP.40: Minimize context switching
entries.summary: Context switches are expensive.

entries.title: CP.41: Minimize thread creation and destruction
entries.summary: Thread creation is expensive.

entries.title: CP.42: Don't `wait` without a condition
entries.summary: A `wait` without a condition can miss a wakeup or wake up simply to find that there is no work to do.

entries.title: CP.43: Minimize time spent in a critical section
entries.summary: The less time is spent with a `mutex` taken, the less chance that another `thread` has to wait,

entries.title: CP.44: Remember to name your `lock_guard`s and `unique_lock`s
entries.summary: An unnamed local object is a temporary that immediately goes out of scope.

entries.title: CP.50: Define a `mutex` together with the data it guards. Use `synchronized_value` where possible
entries.summary: It should be obvious to a reader that the data is to be guarded and how. This decreases the chance of the wrong mutex being locked, or the mutex not being locked.

entries.title: CP.coro: Coroutines
entries.summary: This section focuses on uses of coroutines.

entries.title: CP.51: Do not use capturing lambdas that are coroutines
entries.summary: Usage patterns that are correct with normal lambdas are hazardous with coroutine lambdas. The obvious pattern of capturing variables will result in accessing freed memory after the first suspension po

entries.title: CP.52: Do not hold locks or other synchronization primitives across suspension points
entries.summary: This pattern creates a significant risk of deadlocks.  Some types of waits will allow the current thread to perform additional work until the asynchronous operation has completed. If the thread holdin

entries.title: CP.53: Parameters to coroutines should not be passed by reference
entries.summary: Once a coroutine reaches the first suspension point, such as a co_await, the synchronous portion returns. After that point any parameters passed by reference are dangling. Any usage beyond that is und

entries.title: CP.par: Parallelism
entries.summary: By "parallelism" we refer to performing a task (more or less) simultaneously ("in parallel with") on many data items.

entries.title: CP.mess: Message passing
entries.summary: The standard-library facilities are quite low-level, focused on the needs of close-to-the-hardware critical programming using `thread`s, `mutex`es, `atomic` types, etc.

entries.title: CP.60: Use a `future` to return a value from a concurrent task
entries.summary: A `future` preserves the usual function call return semantics for asynchronous tasks.

entries.title: CP.61: Use `async()` to spawn concurrent tasks
entries.summary: Similar to [R.12](#rr-immediate-alloc), which tells you to avoid raw owning pointers, you should

entries.title: CP.vec: Vectorization
entries.summary: Vectorization is a technique for executing a number of tasks concurrently without introducing explicit synchronization.

entries.title: CP.free: Lock-free programming
entries.summary: Synchronization using `mutex`es and `condition_variable`s can be relatively expensive.

entries.title: CP.100: Don't use lock-free programming unless you absolutely have to
entries.summary: It's error-prone and requires expert level knowledge of language features, machine architecture, and data structures.

entries.title: CP.101: Distrust your hardware/compiler combination
entries.summary: The low-level hardware interfaces used by lock-free programming are among the hardest to implement well and among

entries.title: CP.102: Carefully study the literature
entries.summary: With the exception of atomics and a few other standard patterns, lock-free programming is really an expert-only topic.

entries.title: CP.110: Do not write your own double-checked locking for initialization
entries.summary: Since C++11, static local variables are now initialized in a thread-safe way. When combined with the RAII pattern, static local variables can replace the need for writing your own double-checked locki

entries.title: CP.111: Use a conventional pattern if you really need double-checked locking
entries.summary: Double-checked locking is easy to mess up. If you really need to write your own double-checked locking, in spite of the rules [CP.110: Do not write your own double-checked locking for initialization](

entries.title: CP.etc: Etc. concurrency rules
entries.summary: These rules defy simple categorization:

entries.title: CP.200: Use `volatile` only to talk to non-C++ memory
entries.summary: `volatile` is used to refer to objects that are shared with "non-C++" code or hardware that does not follow the C++ memory model.

entries.title: CP.201: ??? Signals
entries.summary: ???UNIX signal handling???. Might be worth reminding how little is async-signal-safe, and how to communicate with a signal handler (best is probably "not at all")

entries.title: E.1: Develop an error-handling strategy early in a design
entries.summary: A consistent and complete strategy for handling errors and resource leaks is hard to retrofit into a system.

entries.title: E.2: Throw an exception to signal that a function can't perform its assigned task
entries.summary: To make error handling systematic, robust, and non-repetitive.

entries.title: E.3: Use exceptions for error handling only
entries.summary: To keep error handling separated from "ordinary code."

entries.title: E.4: Design your error-handling strategy around invariants
entries.summary: To use an object it must be in a valid state (defined formally or informally by an invariant) and to recover from an error every object not destroyed must be in a valid state.

entries.title: E.5: Let a constructor establish an invariant, and throw if it cannot
entries.summary: Leaving an object without its invariant established is asking for trouble.

entries.title: E.6: Use RAII to prevent leaks
entries.summary: Leaks are typically unacceptable.

entries.title: E.7: State your preconditions
entries.summary: To avoid interface errors.

entries.title: E.8: State your postconditions
entries.summary: To avoid interface errors.

entries.title: E.12: Use `noexcept` when exiting a function because of a `throw` is impossible or unacceptable
entries.summary: To make error handling systematic, robust, and efficient.

entries.title: E.13: Never throw while being the direct owner of an object
entries.summary: That would be a leak.

entries.title: E.14: Use purpose-designed user-defined types as exceptions (not built-in types)
entries.summary: A user-defined type can better transmit information about an error to a handler.  Information

entries.title: E.15: Throw by value, catch exceptions from a hierarchy by reference
entries.summary: Throwing by value (not by pointer) and catching by reference prevents copying, especially slicing base subobjects.

entries.title: E.16: Destructors, deallocation, `swap`, and exception type copy/move construction must never fail
entries.summary: We don't know how to write reliable programs if a destructor, a swap, a memory deallocation, or attempting to copy/move-construct an exception object fails; that is, if it exits by an exception or sim

entries.title: E.17: Don't try to catch every exception in every function
entries.summary: Catching an exception in a function that cannot take a meaningful recovery action leads to complexity and waste.

entries.title: E.18: Minimize the use of explicit `try`/`catch`
entries.summary: `try`/`catch` is verbose and non-trivial uses are error-prone.

entries.title: E.19: Use a `final_action` object to express cleanup if no suitable resource handle is available
entries.summary: `finally` from the [GSL](#gsl-guidelines-support-library) is less verbose and harder to get wrong than `try`/`catch`.

entries.title: E.25: If you can't throw exceptions, simulate RAII for resource management
entries.summary: Even without exceptions, [RAII](#re-raii) is usually the best and most systematic way of dealing with resources.

entries.title: E.26: If you can't throw exceptions, consider failing fast
entries.summary: If you can't do a good job at recovering, at least you can get out before too much consequential damage is done.

entries.title: E.27: If you can't throw exceptions, use error codes systematically
entries.summary: Systematic use of any error-handling strategy minimizes the chance of forgetting to handle an error.

entries.title: E.28: Avoid error handling based on global state (e.g. `errno`)
entries.summary: Global state is hard to manage and it is easy to forget to check it.

entries.title: E.30: Don't use exception specifications
entries.summary: Exception specifications make error handling brittle, impose a run-time cost, and have been removed from the C++ standard.

entries.title: E.31: Properly order your `catch`-clauses
entries.summary: `catch`-clauses are evaluated in the order they appear and one clause can hide another.

entries.title: Con.1: By default, make objects immutable
entries.summary: Immutable objects are easier to reason about, so make objects non-`const` only when there is a need to change their value.

entries.title: Con.2: By default, make member functions `const`
entries.summary: A member function should be marked `const` unless it changes the object's observable state.

entries.title: Con.3: By default, pass pointers and references to `const`s
entries.summary: To avoid a called function unexpectedly changing the value.

entries.title: Con.4: Use `const` to define objects with values that do not change after construction
entries.summary: Prevent surprises from unexpectedly changed object values.

entries.title: Con.5: Use `constexpr` for values that can be computed at compile time
entries.summary: Better performance, better compile-time checking, guaranteed compile-time evaluation, no possibility of race conditions.

entries.title: T.gp: Generic programming
entries.summary: Generic programming is programming using types and algorithms parameterized by types, values, and algorithms.

entries.title: T.1: Use templates to raise the level of abstraction of code
entries.summary: Generality. Reuse. Efficiency. Encourages consistent definition of user types.

entries.title: T.2: Use templates to express algorithms that apply to many argument types
entries.summary: Generality. Minimizing the amount of source code. Interoperability. Reuse.

entries.title: T.3: Use templates to express containers and ranges
entries.summary: Containers need an element type, and expressing that as a template argument is general, reusable, and type safe.

entries.title: T.4: Use templates to express syntax tree manipulation
T.5: Combine generic and OO techniques to amplify their strengths, not their costs
entries.summary: Generic and OO techniques are complementary.

entries.title: T.concepts: Concept rules
entries.summary: Concepts is a C++20 facility for specifying requirements for template arguments.

entries.title: T.10: Specify concepts for all template arguments
entries.summary: Correctness and readability.

entries.title: T.11: Whenever possible use standard concepts
entries.summary: "Standard" concepts (as provided by the [GSL](#gsl-guidelines-support-library) and the ISO standard itself)

entries.title: T.12: Prefer concept names over `auto` for local variables
entries.summary: `auto` is the weakest concept. Concept names convey more meaning than just `auto`.

entries.title: T.13: Prefer the shorthand notation for simple, single-type argument concepts
entries.summary: Readability. Direct expression of an idea.

entries.title: T.concepts.def: Concept definition rules
entries.summary: Defining good concepts is non-trivial.

entries.title: T.20: Avoid "concepts" without meaningful semantics
entries.summary: Concepts are meant to express semantic notions, such as "a number", "a range" of elements, and "totally ordered."

entries.title: T.21: Require a complete set of operations for a concept
entries.summary: Ease of comprehension.

entries.title: T.22: Specify axioms for concepts
entries.summary: A meaningful/useful concept has a semantic meaning.

entries.title: T.23: Differentiate a refined concept from its more general case by adding new use patterns.
entries.summary: Otherwise they cannot be distinguished automatically by the compiler.

entries.title: T.24: Use tag classes or traits to differentiate concepts that differ only in semantics.
entries.summary: Two concepts requiring the same syntax but having different semantics lead to ambiguity unless the programmer differentiates them.

entries.title: T.25: Avoid complementary constraints
entries.summary: Clarity. Maintainability.

entries.title: T.26: Prefer to define concepts in terms of use-patterns rather than simple syntax
entries.summary: The definition is more readable and corresponds directly to what a user has to write.

entries.title: Template interfaces
entries.summary: Over the years, programming with templates has suffered from a weak distinction between the interface of a template

entries.title: T.40: Use function objects to pass operations to algorithms
entries.summary: Function objects can carry more information through an interface than a "plain" pointer to function.

entries.title: T.41: Require only essential properties in a template's concepts
entries.summary: Keep interfaces simple and stable.

entries.title: T.42: Use template aliases to simplify notation and hide implementation details
entries.summary: Improved readability.

entries.title: T.43: Prefer `using` over `typedef` for defining aliases
entries.summary: Improved readability: With `using`, the new name comes first rather than being embedded somewhere in a declaration.

entries.title: T.44: Use function templates to deduce class template argument types (where feasible)
entries.summary: Writing the template argument types explicitly can be tedious and unnecessarily verbose.

entries.title: T.47: Avoid highly visible unconstrained templates with common names
entries.summary: An unconstrained template argument is a perfect match for anything so such a template can be preferred over more specific types that require minor conversions.

entries.title: T.48: If your compiler does not support concepts, fake them with `enable_if`
entries.summary: Because that's the best we can do without direct concept support.

entries.title: T.49: Where possible, avoid type-erasure
entries.summary: Type erasure incurs an extra level of indirection by hiding type information behind a separate compilation boundary.

entries.title: T.def: Template definitions
entries.summary: A template definition (class or function) can contain arbitrary code, so only a comprehensive review of C++ programming techniques would cover this topic.

entries.title: T.60: Minimize a template's context dependencies
entries.summary: Eases understanding.

entries.title: T.61: Do not over-parameterize members (SCARY)
entries.summary: A member that does not depend on a template parameter cannot be used except for a specific template argument.

entries.title: T.62: Place non-dependent class template members in a non-templated base class
entries.summary: Allow the base class members to be used without specifying template arguments and without template instantiation.

entries.title: T.64: Use specialization to provide alternative implementations of class templates
entries.summary: A template defines a general interface.

entries.title: T.65: Use tag dispatch to provide alternative implementations of a function
entries.summary: * A template defines a general interface.

entries.title: T.67: Use specialization to provide alternative implementations for irregular types
T.68: Use `{}` rather than `()` within templates to avoid ambiguities
entries.summary: `()` is vulnerable to grammar ambiguities.

entries.title: T.69: Inside a template, don't make an unqualified non-member function call unless you intend it to be a customization point
entries.summary: * Provide only intended flexibility.

entries.title: T.temp-hier: Template and hierarchy rules:
entries.summary: Templates are the backbone of C++'s support for generic programming and class hierarchies the backbone of its support

entries.title: T.80: Do not naively templatize a class hierarchy
entries.summary: Templating a class hierarchy that has many functions, especially many virtual functions, can lead to code bloat.

entries.title: T.81: Do not mix hierarchies and arrays
entries.summary: An array of derived classes can implicitly "decay" to a pointer to a base class with potential disastrous results.

entries.title: T.82: Linearize a hierarchy when virtual functions are undesirable
T.83: Do not declare a member function template virtual
entries.summary: C++ does not support that.

entries.title: T.84: Use a non-template core implementation to provide an ABI-stable interface
entries.summary: Improve stability of code.

entries.title: T.var: Variadic template rules
T.100: Use variadic templates when you need a function that takes a variable number of arguments of a variety of types
entries.summary: Variadic templates is the most general mechanism for that, and is both efficient and type-safe. Don't use C varargs.

entries.title: T.101: ??? How to pass arguments to a variadic template ???
T.102: How to process arguments to a variadic template
T.103: Don't use variadic templates for homogeneous argument lists
entries.summary: There are more precise ways of specifying a homogeneous sequence, such as an `initializer_list`.

entries.title: T.meta: Template metaprogramming (TMP)
entries.summary: Templates provide a general mechanism for compile-time programming.

entries.title: T.120: Use template metaprogramming only when you really need to
entries.summary: Template metaprogramming is hard to get right, slows down compilation, and is often very hard to maintain.

entries.title: T.121: Use template metaprogramming primarily to emulate concepts
entries.summary: Where C++20 is not available, we need to emulate them using TMP.

entries.title: T.122: Use templates (usually template aliases) to compute types at compile time
entries.summary: Template metaprogramming is the only directly supported and half-way principled way of generating types at compile time.

entries.title: T.123: Use `constexpr` functions to compute values at compile time
entries.summary: A function is the most obvious and conventional way of expressing the computation of a value.

entries.title: T.124: Prefer to use standard-library TMP facilities
entries.summary: Facilities defined in the standard, such as `conditional`, `enable_if`, and `tuple`, are portable and can be assumed to be known.

entries.title: T.125: If you need to go beyond the standard-library TMP facilities, use an existing library
entries.summary: Getting advanced TMP facilities is not easy and using a library makes you part of a (hopefully supportive) community.

entries.title: T.140: If an operation can be reused, give it a name
entries.summary: See [F.10](#rf-name)

entries.title: T.141: Use an unnamed lambda if you need a simple function object in one place only
entries.summary: See [F.11](#rf-lambda)

entries.title: T.142?: Use template variables to simplify notation
entries.summary: Improved readability.

entries.title: T.143: Don't write unintentionally non-generic code
entries.summary: Generality. Reusability. Don't gratuitously commit to details; use the most general facilities available.

entries.title: T.144: Don't specialize function templates
entries.summary: You can't partially specialize a function template per language rules. You can fully specialize a function template but you almost certainly want to overload instead -- because function template speci

entries.title: T.150: Check that a class matches a concept using `static_assert`
entries.summary: If you intend for a class to match a concept, verifying that early saves users' pain.

entries.title: CPL.1: Prefer C++ to C
entries.summary: C++ provides better type checking and more notational support.

entries.title: CPL.2: If you must use C, use the common subset of C and C++, and compile the C code as C++
entries.summary: That subset can be compiled with both C and C++ compilers, and when compiled as C++ is better type checked than "pure C."

entries.title: CPL.3: If you must use C for interfaces, use C++ in the calling code using such interfaces
entries.summary: C++ is more expressive than C and offers better support for many types of programming.

entries.title: SF.1: Use a `.cpp` suffix for code files and `.h` for interface files if your project doesn't already follow another convention
entries.summary: See [NL.27](#rl-file-suffix)

entries.title: SF.2: A header file must not contain object definitions or non-inline function definitions
entries.summary: Including entities subject to the one-definition rule leads to linkage errors.

entries.title: SF.3: Use header files for all declarations used in multiple source files
entries.summary: Maintainability. Readability.

entries.title: SF.4: Include header files before other declarations in a file
