entries.summary: Minimize context dependencies and increase readability.

entries.title: SF.5: A `.cpp` file must include the header file(s) that defines its interface
entries.summary: This enables the compiler to do an early consistency check.

entries.title: SF.6: Use `using namespace` directives for transition, for foundation libraries (such as `std`), or within a local scope (only)
entries.summary: `using namespace` can lead to name clashes, so it should be used sparingly.

entries.title: SF.7: Don't write `using namespace` at global scope in a header file
entries.summary: Doing so takes away an `#include`r's ability to effectively disambiguate and to use alternatives. It also makes `#include`d headers order-dependent as they might have different meaning when included i

entries.title: SF.8: Use `#include` guards for all header files
entries.summary: To avoid files being `#include`d several times.

entries.title: SF.9: Avoid cyclic dependencies among source files
entries.summary: Cycles complicate comprehension and slow down compilation. They also

entries.title: SF.10: Avoid dependencies on implicitly `#include`d names
entries.summary: Avoid surprises.

entries.title: SF.11: Header files should be self-contained
entries.summary: Usability, headers should be simple to use and work when included on their own.

entries.title: SF.12: Prefer the quoted form of `#include` for files relative to the including file and the angle bracket form everywhere else
entries.summary: The [standard](https://eel.is/c++draft/cpp.include) provides flexibility for compilers to implement

entries.title: SF.13: Use portable header identifiers in `#include` statements
entries.summary: The [standard](https://eel.is/c++draft/cpp.include) does not specify how compilers uniquely locate headers from an identifier in an `#include` directive, nor does it specify what constitutes uniquenes

entries.title: SF.20: Use `namespace`s to express logical structure
SF.21: Don't use an unnamed (anonymous) namespace in a header
entries.summary: It is almost always a bug to mention an unnamed namespace in a header file.

entries.title: SF.22: Use an unnamed (anonymous) namespace for all internal/non-exported entities
entries.summary: Nothing external can depend on an entity in a nested unnamed namespace.

entries.title: SL.1:  Use libraries wherever possible
entries.summary: Save time. Don't re-invent the wheel.

entries.title: SL.2: Prefer the standard library to other libraries
entries.summary: More people know the standard library.

entries.title: SL.3: Do not add non-standard entities to namespace `std`
entries.summary: Adding to `std` might change the meaning of otherwise standards conforming code.

entries.title: SL.4: Use the standard library in a type-safe manner
entries.summary: Because, obviously, breaking this rule can lead to undefined behavior, memory corruption, and all kinds of other bad errors.

entries.title: SL.con: Containers
SL.con.1: Prefer using STL `array` or `vector` instead of a C array
entries.summary: C arrays are less safe, and have no advantages over `array` and `vector`.

entries.title: SL.con.2: Prefer using STL `vector` by default unless you have a reason to use a different container
entries.summary: `vector` and `array` are the only standard containers that offer the following advantages:

entries.title: SL.con.3: Avoid bounds errors
entries.summary: Read or write beyond an allocated range of elements typically leads to bad errors, wrong results, crashes, and security violations.

entries.title: SL.con.4: don't use `memset` or `memcpy` for arguments that are not trivially-copyable
entries.summary: Doing so messes the semantics of the objects (e.g., by overwriting a `vptr`).

entries.title: SL.str: String
entries.summary: Text manipulation is a huge topic.

entries.title: SL.str.1: Use `std::string` to own character sequences
entries.summary: `string` correctly handles allocation, ownership, copying, gradual expansion, and offers a variety of useful operations.

entries.title: SL.str.2: Use `std::string_view` or `gsl::span` to refer to character sequences
entries.summary: `std::string_view` or `gsl::span<char>` provides simple and (potentially) safe access to character sequences independently of how

entries.title: SL.str.3: Use `zstring` or `czstring` to refer to a C-style, zero-terminated, sequence of characters
entries.summary: Readability.

entries.title: SL.str.4: Use `char*` to refer to a single character
entries.summary: The variety of uses of `char*` in current code is a major source of errors.

entries.title: SL.str.5: Use `std::byte` to refer to byte values that do not necessarily represent characters
entries.summary: Use of `char*` to represent a pointer to something that is not necessarily a character causes confusion

entries.title: SL.str.10: Use `std::string` when you need to perform locale-sensitive string operations
entries.summary: `std::string` supports standard-library [`locale` facilities](#rstr-locale)

entries.title: SL.str.11: Use `gsl::span` rather than `std::string_view` when you need to mutate a string
entries.summary: `std::string_view` is read-only.

entries.title: SL.str.12: Use the `s` suffix for string literals meant to be standard-library `string`s
entries.summary: Direct expression of an idea minimizes mistakes.

entries.title: SL.io: Iostream
entries.summary: `iostream`s is a type safe, extensible, formatted and unformatted I/O library for streaming I/O.

entries.title: SL.io.1: Use character-level input only when you have to
entries.summary: Unless you genuinely just deal with individual characters, using character-level input leads to the user code performing potentially error-prone

entries.title: SL.io.2: When reading, always consider ill-formed input
entries.summary: Errors are typically best handled as soon as possible.

entries.title: SL.io.3: Prefer `iostream`s for I/O
entries.summary: `iostream`s are safe, flexible, and extensible.

entries.title: SL.io.10: Unless you use `printf`-family functions call `ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false)`
entries.summary: Synchronizing `iostreams` with `printf-style` I/O can be costly.

entries.title: SL.io.50: Avoid `endl`
entries.summary: The `endl` manipulator is mostly equivalent to `'\n'` and `"\n"`;

entries.title: SL.regex: Regex
entries.summary: `<regex>` is the standard C++ regular expression library.

entries.title: SL.chrono: Time
entries.summary: `<chrono>` (defined in namespace `std::chrono`) provides the notions of `time_point` and `duration` together with functions for

entries.title: SL.C: The C Standard Library
SL.C.1: Don't use setjmp/longjmp
entries.summary: a `longjmp` ignores destructors, thus invalidating all resource-management strategies relying on RAII

entries.title: A.1: Separate stable code from less stable code
entries.summary: Isolating less stable code facilitates its unit testing, interface improvement, refactoring, and eventual deprecation.

entries.title: A.2: Express potentially reusable parts as a library
entries.summary: A library is a collection of declarations and definitions maintained, documented, and shipped together.

entries.title: A.4: There should be no cycles among libraries
entries.summary: * A cycle complicates the build process.

entries.title: NR.1: Don't insist that all declarations should be at the top of a function
entries.summary: The "all declarations on top" rule is a legacy of old programming languages that didn't allow initialization of variables and constants after a statement.

entries.title: NR.2: Don't insist on having only a single `return`-statement in a function
entries.summary: The single-return rule can lead to unnecessarily convoluted code and the introduction of extra state variables.

entries.title: NR.3: Don't avoid exceptions
entries.summary: There seem to be four main reasons given for not using exceptions:

entries.title: NR.4: Don't insist on placing each class definition in its own source file
entries.summary: The resulting number of files from placing each class in its own file are hard to manage and can slow down compilation.

entries.title: NR.5: Don't use two-phase initialization
entries.summary: Splitting initialization into two leads to weaker invariants,

entries.title: NR.6: Don't place all cleanup actions at the end of a function and `goto exit`
entries.summary: `goto` is error-prone.

entries.title: NR.7: Don't make data members `protected`
entries.summary: `protected` data is a source of errors.

entries.title: RF.core: Core Guidelines materials
entries.summary: This section contains materials that have been useful for presenting the core guidelines and the ideas behind them:

entries.title: Acknowledgements
entries.summary: Thanks to the many people who contributed rules, suggestions, supporting information, references, etc.:

entries.title: Pro.safety: Type-safety profile
entries.summary: This profile makes it easier to construct code that uses types correctly and avoids inadvertent type punning.

entries.title: Pro.bounds: Bounds safety profile
entries.summary: This profile makes it easier to construct code that operates within the bounds of allocated blocks of memory.

entries.title: Pro.lifetime: Lifetime safety profile
entries.summary: Accessing through a pointer that doesn't point to anything is a major source of errors,

entries.title: GSL.view: Views
entries.summary: These types allow the user to distinguish between owning and non-owning pointers and between pointers to a single object and pointers to the first element of a sequence.

entries.title: GSL.owner: Ownership pointers
entries.summary: * `unique_ptr<T>`     // unique ownership: `std::unique_ptr<T>`

entries.title: GSL.assert: Assertions
entries.summary: * `Expects`     // precondition assertion. Currently placed in function bodies. Later, should be moved to declarations.

entries.title: GSL.util: Utilities
entries.summary: * `finally`        // `finally(f)` makes a `final_action{f}` with a destructor that invokes `f`

entries.title: GSL.concept: Concepts
entries.summary: These concepts (type predicates) are borrowed from

entries.title: GSL.ptr: Smart pointer concepts
entries.summary: * `Pointer`  // A type with `*`, `->`, `==`, and default construction (default construction is assumed to set the singular "null" value)

entries.title: NL.1: Don't say in comments what can be clearly stated in code
entries.summary: Compilers do not read comments.

entries.title: NL.2: State intent in comments
entries.summary: Code says what is done, not what is supposed to be done. Often intent can be stated more clearly and concisely than the implementation.

entries.title: NL.3: Keep comments crisp
entries.summary: Verbosity slows down understanding and makes the code harder to read by spreading it around in the source file.

entries.title: NL.4: Maintain a consistent indentation style
entries.summary: Readability. Avoidance of "silly mistakes."

entries.title: NL.5: Avoid encoding type information in names
entries.summary: If names reflect types rather than functionality, it becomes hard to change the types used to provide that functionality.

entries.title: NL.7: Make the length of a name roughly proportional to the length of its scope
entries.summary: **Rationale**: The larger the scope the greater the chance of confusion and of an unintended name clash.

entries.title: NL.8: Use a consistent naming style
entries.summary: **Rationale**: Consistency in naming and naming style increases readability.

entries.title: NL.9: Use `ALL_CAPS` for macro names only
entries.summary: To avoid confusing macros with names that obey scope and type rules.

entries.title: NL.10: Prefer `underscore_style` names
entries.summary: The use of underscores to separate parts of a name is the original C and C++ style and used in the C++ Standard Library.

entries.title: NL.11: Make literals readable
entries.summary: Readability.

entries.title: NL.15: Use spaces sparingly
entries.summary: Too much space makes the text larger and distracts.

entries.title: NL.16: Use a conventional class member declaration order
entries.summary: A conventional order of members improves readability.

entries.title: NL.17: Use K&R-derived layout
entries.summary: This is the original C and C++ layout. It preserves vertical space well. It distinguishes different language constructs (such as functions and classes) well.

entries.title: NL.18: Use C++-style declarator layout
entries.summary: The C-style layout emphasizes use in expressions and grammar, whereas the C++-style emphasizes types.

entries.title: NL.19: Avoid names that are easily misread
entries.summary: Readability.

entries.title: NL.20: Don't place two statements on the same line
entries.summary: Readability.

entries.title: NL.21: Declare one name (only) per declaration
entries.summary: Readability.

entries.title: NL.25: Don't use `void` as an argument type
entries.summary: It's verbose and only needed where C compatibility matters.

entries.title: NL.26: Use conventional `const` notation
entries.summary: Conventional notation is more familiar to more programmers.

entries.title: NL.27: Use a `.cpp` suffix for code files and `.h` for interface files
entries.summary: It's a longstanding convention.

entries.summary: The initial primary authors and maintainers are Bjarne Stroustrup and Herb Sutter, and the guidelines so far were developed with contributions from experts at CERN, Microsoft, Morgan Stanley, and seve

entries.summary: See [CONTRIBUTING.md](https://github.com/isocpp/CppCoreGuidelines/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md). We appreciate volunteer help!

entries.title: FAQ.5: How can I become an editor/maintainer?
entries.summary: By contributing a lot first and having the consistent quality of your contributions recognized. See [CONTRIBUTING.md](https://github.com/isocpp/CppCoreGuidelines/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md). We appre

entries.summary: No. These guidelines are outside the standard. They are intended to serve the standard, and be maintained as current guidelines about how to use the current Standard C++ effectively. We aim to keep th

entries.summary: Because `isocpp` is the Standard C++ Foundation; the committee's repositories are under [github.com/*cplusplus*](https://github.com/cplusplus). Some neutral organization has to own the copyright and l

entries.title: FAQ.8: Will there be a C++98 version of these Guidelines? A C++11 version?
entries.summary: No. These guidelines are about how to best use modern standard C++ and write code assuming you have a modern conforming compiler.

entries.title: FAQ.9: Do these guidelines propose new language features?
entries.summary: No. These guidelines are about how to best use modern Standard C++, and they limit themselves to recommending only those features.

entries.title: FAQ.10: What version of Markdown do these guidelines use?
entries.summary: These coding standards are written using [CommonMark](https://commonmark.org), and `<a>` HTML anchors.

entries.title: FAQ.52: Why not supply an actual GSL implementation in/with these guidelines?
entries.summary: We are reluctant to bless one particular implementation because we do not want to make people think there is only one, and inadvertently stifle parallel implementations. And if these guidelines includ

entries.title: FAQ.53: Why weren't the GSL types proposed through Boost?
entries.summary: Because we want to use them immediately, and because they are temporary in that we want to retire them as soon as types that fill the same needs exist in the standard library.

entries.title: FAQ.54: Has the GSL (guidelines support library) been approved by the ISO C++ standards committee?
entries.summary: No. The GSL exists only to supply a few types and aliases that are not currently in the standard library. If the committee decides on standardized versions (of these or other types that fill the same

entries.title: FAQ.55: If you're using the standard types where available, why is the GSL `span` different from the `string_view` in the Library Fundamentals 1 Technical Specification and C++17 Working Paper? Why not just use the committee-approved `string_view`?
entries.summary: The consensus on the taxonomy of views for the C++ Standard Library was that "view" means "read-only", and "span" means "read/write". If you only need a read-only view of characters that does not need

entries.title: FAQ.56: Is `owner` the same as the proposed `observer_ptr`?
entries.summary: No. `owner` owns, is an alias, and can be applied to any indirection type. The main intent of `observer_ptr` is to signify a *non*-owning pointer.

entries.title: FAQ.57: Is `stack_array` the same as the standard `array`?
entries.summary: No. `stack_array` is guaranteed to be allocated on the stack. Although a `std::array` contains its storage directly inside itself, the `array` object can be put anywhere, including the heap.

entries.title: FAQ.58: Is `dyn_array` the same as `vector` or the proposed `dynarray`?
entries.summary: No. `dyn_array` is a container, like `vector`, but it is not resizable; its size is fixed at runtime when it is constructed.

entries.title: FAQ.59: Is `Expects` the same as `assert`?
entries.summary: No. It is a placeholder for language support for contract preconditions.

entries.title: FAQ.60: Is `Ensures` the same as `assert`?
entries.summary: No. It is a placeholder for language support for contract postconditions.

entries.title: Discussion: Usage of noexcept
Discussion: Destructors, deallocation, and swap must never fail
entries.summary: Never allow an error to be reported from a destructor, a resource deallocation function (e.g., `operator delete`), or a `swap` function using `throw`. It is nearly impossible to write useful code if t

entries.title: Define Copy, move, and destroy consistently
Discussion: Provide strong resource safety; that is, never leak anything that you think of as a resource
entries.summary: Prevent leaks. Leaks can lead to performance degradation, mysterious error, system crashes, and security violations.

entries.title: Discussion: Never return or throw while holding a resource not owned by a handle
entries.summary: That would be a leak.

entries.title: Discussion: A "raw" pointer or reference is never a resource handle
entries.summary: To be able to distinguish owners from views.

entries.title: Discussion: Never let a pointer outlive the object it points to
entries.summary: To avoid extremely hard-to-find errors. Dereferencing such a pointer is undefined behavior and could lead to violations of the type system.

entries.title: Discussion: Use templates to express containers (and other resource handles)
entries.summary: To provide statically type-safe manipulation of elements.

entries.title: Discussion: Return containers by value (relying on move or copy elision for efficiency)
entries.summary: To simplify code and eliminate a need for explicit memory management. To bring an object into a surrounding scope, thereby extending its lifetime.

entries.title: Discussion: If a class is a resource handle, it needs a constructor, a destructor, and copy and/or move operations
entries.summary: To provide complete control of the lifetime of the resource. To provide a coherent set of operations on the resource.

entries.title: Discussion: If a class is a container, give it an initializer-list constructor
entries.summary: It is common to need an initial set of elements.

entries.title: Tools: [Clang-tidy](https://clang.llvm.org/extra/clang-tidy/checks/list.html)
entries.summary: Clang-tidy has a set of rules that specifically enforce the C++ Core Guidelines. These rules are named in the pattern `cppcoreguidelines-*`.

entries.title: Tools: [CppCoreCheck](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/visualstudio/code-quality/using-the-cpp-core-guidelines-checkers)
entries.summary: The Microsoft compiler's C++ code analysis contains a set of rules specifically aimed at enforcement of the C++ Core Guidelines.
